speaker: interviewer
text: And whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Isabel Alcoff. I'll be conducting an interview with Ruth Weitz on February 9, 1997. We're in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, and the language is English. Good morning. Can you tell me your full name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ruth Weitz-- R-U-T-H W-E-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1922, May the 29th.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pohorylce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to spell. Spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, OK. We'll spell it later then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me which country was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Ukraine, Poland, Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you tell me who your parents were-- the parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents was Saul Weitz and Sarah Weitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Father Saul Weitz and mother Sara Weitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, your maiden name is the same as your married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never changed my maiden name. I was married to a Weitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband's name was Weitz, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Weitz, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fourth generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Can you tell me a little bit about your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was raised up a farm. My father used to buy cattles to the slaughter house. My mother was a housewife. And I was a spoiled brat. I can tell you that. And there was walking, playing, going to school. Went public school and Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Can you tell me how big your house was then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a small house. We used to have a room. I used to have with my sister a room, beautiful furniture, handmade furniture my mother done-- that arranged. And my father and mother-- was a nice, livable house. We have a stables for the horses and cows. We have a stable for the crops. And we have that. We have a helps. My mother have a maid, and my father have a-- here, you call it butler. There, you used to call a helper worker. And used to have one guy to the horses and one guy to help him drive the cattles when he was going shopping.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, your-- in your home, who else lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived my mother and father, me, and my sister and brother, and the guy what was working for us lived. The maid came every morning. My mother no liked it that she should sleep home.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. What were your sister and brother's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Radze in Jewish, and my brother was Harold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was born in 1926, May 15. And my brother was 1929, August 28.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was that like in your house? Can you describe, like, a typical day in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's typical day, we went in the morning to school. First, I was the first grade. I went after new public school. And in the morning, I used to go in Hebrew school, came home, played with the kids. I mean, gentle kids we played. We have a good friend who was very nice. And that was a typical day. Came Friday night, my mother light candles. We're not supposed to do nothing. Saturday, my father went to synagogue, came home. We have a lunch. Dinner Saturday night-- when I got older, I used to go with the girls out for walk, to see what's going on and played with the girl-- you know, walking around, enjoying ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother make a special meal for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she make?

speaker: interviewee
text: She make chicken soup. She made roast beef. She make delicious noodle pudding. She make rice pudding, where I can never make the same. I make a lots of her dishes, but not-- that the only dish I cannot make it. And then she used to come Saturday for lunch. Company from the shul we used to have with my mother. And my father brought every time somebody home from shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And describe how holidays were spent.

speaker: interviewee
text: The holidays, we was going in shul. Right, and Jewish New Year's, my mother used to go in shul dressed up, and her hat, gorgeous. And my father went in shul. Came to Yom Kippur night, [INAUDIBLE]. We was sitting. All he went all around the table, kissed us on forehead. And he used to say, we know what kind of year we have this year, but we don't know what happen next year. We pray to God we have a better year. That was my father used to say every time. Time to breaking the fast, I went home earlier and prepared of the table. She used to bake a lot-- strudels, cookies, coffee cake. And she used to have a chicken drumstick prepared for breaking the fast. And we used to have like 10-20 people every breaking the holidays. Pesach was, too, very lovely. My mother used to make the ciders, although my father was not too much with the ciders. We used to go to my mother's father. I asked the four questions. We were sitting there. I find the [INAUDIBLE]. My grandpa gave us money. And we went to-- it was very lovely, everything. And they have the holidays twice a year. My mother used to make big dinners. Like the second-- second turn for the holidays-- Pesach-- she used to have the whole family-- it was, like, 40 peoples-- prepared. And I need to help. I was about 12 years old, I remember. I need to stay in kitchen and help to do everything in kitchen. And came peoples. She no have tables, big ones. And she put two boards. I still have on mine the tablecloth, where my mother used it. And we have twice a lot people for dinner. Was like 2, 3 o'clock afternoon. Pesach and Simchat Torah, was the same thing. After Yom Kippur, Simchat Torah, she used to have all the family. Well, my father never liked to go no place to eat. And she need to make the family come together twice a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say the family was invited, and there were about 40 people. Who was there? How big was your family? Were there aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my mother have a sisters. My father have one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father's brother was Mendel Weitz. Her name was Feigel, the aunt. And you have three kids, was Rivche, Shevel, and Aaron, was my father's brother's children. My mother's sister was Malka. And she have three kids, was Henia, Menicha, and Zeche. That was his. And was more family, you know what I mean. And they came together. My mother's brother's children was. I mean, there was Nathan, Ioia, Naphtulim, Morris-- he is Morris now-- Frida. I mean, they camed. And she was every time, she have a lots of-- and we have friends invited. My father, with my mother, we used to throw-- came. And when my brother was born in 1929, for the bris, we three days, was a bris. Celebrate, well, he got the son. He was very excited, my dad. And was for three days, the bris. Was Thursday, born. Was Thursday, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, too, I think. And he celebrate. And of course, very lovely. We lived a normal Jewish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was religious-- half, not completely like my mother and not like my grandpa and grandma. My grandma was very religious, and my grandpa was very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Leiche, my grandma, and my grandpa was Shlim. And I have a great-grandpa, too. My mother's father was Iosl. And he was terrific. He died 1938. It was Yom Kippur and shul. And he came home Sukkot time and he died, when he was 104 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never weared glasses, knowed his prayer plain. I loved him very much. I loved my grandparents-- you know, my mother's father and my mother's mother. But him, well, he was special to me. He give everything he got for me. He gave-- came Hanukkah time, he gave me money. He gave me money to buy what I wanted, not what he wanted. See, that a grandpa was very lovely. But he-- he was, I don't know, old-fashioned, a little bit, maybe. And the funny thing happened. He used to come every Friday, my grandpa-- my mother's father-- home, and he used to-- to our house. Friday afternoon, my mother used to tie his-- trim his beard, trimmed his-- I mean, his hair washed. She used to take care of them. My mother-- see, like, I was the princess by my father. She was the princess by my grandpa. He loved her very much. And he got-- she used to do everything for my grandpa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all these family members live in the same town as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same town, like 2, 3 miles each other. We used to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people get around? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking. No horse and wagon. We have a train go by, you need to go to the station. That time, I took the train, and I went to the city shopping when I was older, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Peremyshlyany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there? What did you buy on a typical--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, typical things-- I'd loved to fancy-- underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry to tell you. You know what I mean? I used to buy nice day slips, you know, silky. I used to buy gorgeous bras-- satin one. Never wear their cotton one. Well, I used to ask my father for money. And he give it to me. And I used to buy-- I used to-- my dresses to make, I need my father. We used to go to the store in that city, used to call [INAUDIBLE], the store. We used to go. And he used to have a lots of clothes-- material. And we used to pay the material, go to the dressmaker, and made the dresses-- shoes, dresses, everything. And I loved that. I loved to go shopping. Until today, I still love to go shopping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. [LAUGHS] That's great. And what did your mother do to keep herself--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a housekeeper. And she was arranged every time to what-- well, we have a lots of field. And we liked to plant potatoes a lot, not-- you know what I mean-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And we have corn for bread. You know the corn-- the wheat, corn-- that we have it. And she arranged people, workers, every morning. And they went to work. Sometime my mother went with them. By 15, I went already with the workers. I took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow enough on the farm for yourselves? Or did you also sell it--

speaker: interviewee
text: We growed stuff. Some stuff we sold-- not the potatoes. Well, the potatoes, we need for the cows. My father used to bring lots of cows home to feed them. And the horses have sugar beats-- you know what I mean that was. But was very lovely, everything. I enjoyed. I still missing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father-- well, describe his business a little bit for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he used to go travel to the city-- from city to city-- buying cows and selling to the slaughterhouses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was he away most of the week?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He can leave in the morning, 8 o'clock, come home 2:00. Sometime he left at 12 o'clock, come home 6:00. But usually was for dinner home in nighttime. Never went nighttime no place.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your younger brother and sister-- what do you remember about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to school. Just-- that was, till the war in 1939, they went going to school, my sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was close with not so-- I was close with my sister but closer with my brother. He was seven years younger, and he was a terrific boy. And I still missing him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why? He used to do a lots of favors for me. Like, I went to go out, and he told-- I give him a quarter, and he can tell any lie. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Lied for me a lots of times. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he have to, Ruth? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Why he have to?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was going some time out, and my mother told me I should be home. And he say, she went to her girlfriend, and leave her alone. Well, I was not by a girlfriend. We went-- like, we used to go see a play. Well, movies was not the electricity. So we went to see plays. And some plays, my mother not agree. Well, she was religious. And she was strict. Is what happened what I wanted to go to see. Is that's why he lied. Sometime when I was late, one time, he catched me kiss-- a boy kissed me. I go tell my-- you mother. And I say, oh no, you not telling. He say, you give me a quarter, I'm not telling.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] So you had a little supply of change for your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: All I need to have it every time, money for my brother. My sister was different. She want to go every place with me. And she was younger four years. And, like, yeah, one time she wanted to put a dress. And my father say, oh no, you're too young for that. You have yours. You wear your. But she, I want her dress. Hers are prettier. And he say, both pretty dresses. And I don't want you to wear it. And that's it. We not allowed to wearing each-- well, I can wearing my mother's shoes. My father never let that. He never let nobody's clothes to wear each other. He was very strict with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all of you children go to school together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, was a one school. We went in school together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there one classroom or different classrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, different classes. Was like, I went to the fourth grade. My sister went to the third-- second grade, was different. She went afternoon. I went in the morning. And my brother went afternoon. You know what I mean? Was different. Well, like, I went in the afternoon Hebrew school. And she went in the morning, Hebrew school, my sister. I mean, we went in Hebrew school. That, we need to attend every day. And one day, I quit. And my mother want to kill me. And I say, I'm not going. I'm too big for that. You know, well, I still took Hebrew nighttime. The Rabbi came to the house and brought about half a dozen boys. And we used to read still Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked Ukrainish, Polish, and Jewish. And that's it first. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And after, when the-- what, the telephone? That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep going. So tell me what you did when you were at home. You spoke three languages. And at school, what language did they teach you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish and Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. All right. And the teachers were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in your classroom, were there are a lot of Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was about half a dozen Jewish children. And the rest was Catholic. Was Ukraine and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have a lot of contact with the non-Jewish kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Was there anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a little bit. We have one teacher was very bad. One teacher-- well, everybody arranged, you know, that she needs to leave. She needs to leave. Well, she was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way? Can you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what way, she came-- the Jewish children, like, we was very good in arithmetic. Well, we took it in Hebrew school. And we was ahead. And she not like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she don't like the lots of things about the Jews. In 1938 was starting to be wars, you know what I mean? Already, you know, Hitler, for 1934, Hitler put in a little bit already that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were feeling the effects of Hitler already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that they used to scream, Jews, go to Israel, you know what I mean? They used to put in 1938 of the stores, not allow the gentile to go in in the Jewish stores. Was starting to be bad in 1938 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's business, that not affect. Well, he was not in the city. The worse was in the city. We in the countries. In the countries, was not so bad still. While the Jews with the gentile kept closer, not so-- and the gentile people make a living from the Jews. Well, they was working of the field and then make money to support themselves. And that was not so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Did you personally have any experiences with anti-Semitism, aside from this teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. And your parents' business was never affected then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And at what point did--

speaker: interviewee
text: You want a glass of water?

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you notice more changes happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it happen 1930-- '38, '39, I think, something like that, starts to change. But we don't paid attention to that. We was going to the plays. We was going dancing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we start-- the only thing started in 1939 were lots of people, Jewish mothers, don't like that the Jewish girl was dancing with the gentile boys. But we started that already, you know what I mean? Like, came out a gentile boy, I knowed when you grow up. And he say very nicely, so I go dancing with him. I can't tell him no. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went dancing. And when I came home, my mother hollered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, Ma, that's OK. Don't worry. He no bite me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever discuss the possibility of leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. There was no talk of-- and did you have contact with any relatives from outside of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We have contact with the United States. Was my uncle's here. My father's brother's here. Was my mother's sister. She wrote just to my grandma, my mother's sister. But my uncle-- he wrote when my grandma died in Pittsburgh. My father's mother died in 1938. And my-- well, she died here in United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had relatives in Pittsburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's why I came to here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. All right. Now, did you know anything about what was going on in Germany? Did you read newspapers? Did you listen to the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard. We started to listen to the radios. And was bad. Started to be bad in 1938, '39. But we don't pay too much attention, was not hitting Poland. When hit 1939, in September 1, and we have a [INAUDIBLE] was not too far. And when I went-- I went to a wedding that Saturday night. Never forget it. Came home about 4 o'clock, changed clothes in a nightgown and went to sleep. 5 o'clock, our windows house were shattered. The German broke that in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it hit-- the first place where he hit is the airport, and was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the mission was burning. And the windowsills were shattered. And my father came into my room. And he said, I think we are in war, children. And that was bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did he-- did he suggest anything? Did he want to change anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We can never change. Well, like, in two weeks, came in the Russian that house. The Germans was coming. But the Russians came in, and I make peace. You know, I mean, half the Russian took Poland, and half took the Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the approximate date that that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- tell you what. Was September, but was Sukkot time, we celebrate. Was the end of September, I think. Sukkot, we celebrate. The Russian came, and we was very happy our place, the liberate the Russians, then the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, how was it with the Russians? Explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, the Russians was lots of people they took of the [INAUDIBLE], what was rich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father went half. He denied what he have it, so much field, so much estate. Maybe he went-- Russian [INAUDIBLE], maybe he would be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But nobody knowed what happened. And we stayed home till 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the schools change at all after the Russian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know why I no went that time in school. My sister was going, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe not. You know what? I don't know what happened. That, I can't remember. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. Tell me, how did your-- did you get to keep your farm under the Russian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the-- the Russian, yeah, we kept still half-- not the whole thing, half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the other half?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other half, took the people. My father give away for the neighbors to take care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you able to maintain your financial status? Were you still pretty well off then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, by the Russians, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father saved a lots of money up. Well, in 1939, I wanted him to build for me a house. OK. And that-- and that money helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to still buying. The Russian used to buy the cows for the slaughterhouse. He still used to do not so big business. But he used to still do that. And we have of the farm. We have the bread, you know, and the corn-- everything. We have a, a mill. And was everything at that time, was still not too bad. But we need to do a lot ourselves, the work. And, like, one neighbor helped the other neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said you wanted your father to build you a house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you getting married, or you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I supposed to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: To who?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a boyfriend I used to have when I was 14, and he was 20. And we used to go steady. And he saved my life. I've been to concentration camp. He took me out, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saul Weitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the hiding wood, he took care of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. All right. We'll talk about that in a minute. So you two were dating-- going steady.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going out. We used to go to  dance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you set a wedding date?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We was not-- I was not setting a wedding day date. But I want everything be perfect. I say, I'm not getting married. And move in not with my mother-in-law, and with not my mother. I say, I want my house. We went shopping, just to got ready the lace for my curtain-- for curtains for the house. We used to have the stone, the bricks-- everything ready. But where my father wanted to build the house, I didn't like the piece of lot. I wanted a different one he should buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
